text: The procedure was performed through a median sternotomy.
Pericardial willis created.
The distal ascending aorta and both superior and inferior vena cavae were cannulated.
And normal thermic cardiopulmonary bypass was initiated.
An ascending erota cardioblesia needle was placed so as a left ventricular vent.
Once the heart is totally decompressed, the main pulmonary artery was divided just proximal to the bifurcation and the pulmonary valve was inspected.
We decided to harvest the autograft on a beating heart.
Using electrocuttery, the pulmonary root was fully mobilized.
A right angle clamp passed through the pulmonary valve into the right ventricular outflow tract.
We marked the area of harvest of the pulmonary root.
Using electrocuttery, right ventriculotomy was performed.
paying attention to the location of the nadir of the pulmonary cusps.
This was done in a circumferential fashion using the electrocuttery all the way around.
It's important to notice the close relationship of the left anterior descending coronary artery and the left main coronary artery to the harvested autograft.
Frequent inspection back and forth
to the pulmonary cusps are critical during the harvest process.
Loose sitting electrocutary is quite hemostatic during the process of autograft harvest.
You can see the medial papillary muscle of the tricuspid valve in view and the base of this medial papillary muscle mark
the location of the first septal perforator branch of the LAD.
Harvesting the autograft posteriorly, it's more of an inoculation process to avoid the first septal perforator.
It's easy to visualize any bleeding from the harvest site at this stage and can be fixed with sutures.

once the autograft is harvested it is trimmed and all the epicardial fat is removed this helps to minimize the bulk of the autograft when we implant it inside the valsalva graft minimal trimming at the proximal cuff muscle is important and the autograft is sized to determine the size of the valsalva graft needed
We usually add 4 mm to the proximal diameter of the pulmonary autograft.
In this patient, we used a 32 mm Falsalva graft.
We trim two-thirds of the skirt of the graft.
Just leaving a couple of rings is enough to secure the proximal end of the pulmonary autograft.
Having the Valsalva graft with the marking lines helps to align with the commissures of the pulmonary autograft.
Using three 5-oproline sutures at each of these commissures helps securing the proximal end of the autograft to the base of the Valsalva graft.
Again, placing these sutures is important not to compromise the base of the
autograft cusps.
We then invert the valsalva graft and secure the distal end of the pulmonia autograft to the valsalva graft using running 5 oproline sutures.
Valve is tested.
Leaving this extra length of the valsalva graft actually is helpful to save one distal suture line.
The next step is to arrest the heart with anti-great cardioplegia.
The ascending aorta is transected and the aortic cusps are resected and the annulus is degraded.
and the left followed by the right coronary arteries are being harvested and the excess native aortic tissue is resected.
This is different than in the regular ROS procedure where we keep the native aortic tissue especially in the area of the non-coronary sinus to support the autograft.
In the current case we don't need to do that because we will have a valsalva graft sitting there.
It is clear the size mismatch and the smaller left ventricular outflow tract, which will require a root enlargement.
We size the left ventricular outflow tract with appropriate Hagar dilator.
In the current patient, we used a combination of posterior and anterior annular enlargement techniques.

Instead of doing a full corneal incision, we do a mini corneal with the incision to the left of the right coronary artery into the septum, which minimize the risk of complete heart block and we will not need any prosthetic patch to close that defect.
Next, we place multiple interrupted, belligerently two oethioprine sutures in a horizontal mattress fashion, and it will pass through the reinforced autograft.

and the auto graft is brought down to the left ventricular outflow tract and secured again the valve is tested any additional hemostatic sutures can be placed at that stage as it will be very difficult to reach this area at the end of the operation

the left coronary artery is then re-implanted usually in the middle of the corresponding sinus of valsalva the re-implantation include all layers of the reinforced autograft the ascending aorta is then resected

and the autograft can be tested again to ensure good hemostasis by infusion of cardioblesia through the graft and we can see the competence and the nice distension of the autograft as well then the valsalva graft is trimmed and we started the reconstruction of the distal aortic anastomosis to the valsalva graft using a running 4-0 proline suture
This is helpful to avoid any twisting on the aortic root after re-implantation of the right coronary artery.
So we save the re-implantation of the right coronary artery at least after we started the distal aortic anastomosis.
The right coronary artery then re-implanted in the middle of the corresponding sinus of the salva in a similar fashion to the left.
and again this three-layer suture line.
If the implantation of the autograft is correct, these coronary arteries typically come into the corresponding sinus of valsalva.
Once this is completed, the distal aortic anastomosis is reconstructed, the heart is deared, and the aortic osculum is removed.
We then used a 30 millimeter cryopreserved pulmonary homograft in the current patient.
The distal anastomosis to the pulmonary arterial confluence was constructed with a running fibrobrone suture.
If there is difficulty visualizing or control the pulmonary arterial confluence, we will do the distal anastomosis prior to completing the aortic anastomosis.

and removing the cross clamp to allow better visualization but in the current patient it was easy to see the distal pulmonary arteries so we decided to do the anastomosis on a beating heart once the pulmonary homograph the distal anastomosis constructed we construct the proximal anastomosis in the current patient despite we had a long pulmonary homograph it was short
So we used a short interposition segment from the Valsalva graft to bridge the gap between the pulmonary homograft and the right ventricular incision.
And the suture line is completed using running for operolene suture.
The patient was then ventilated and weaned off cardiopulmonary bypass without difficulty and was in normal sinus rhythm.
Transits of Yale echocardiogram showed well-preserved biventricular functions, competent neo-aortic and pulmonary homographed valves, widely patent coronary arteries, and good flow in both branches.
